skill.name: azure-upgrade
skill.description: Guides Azure workload upgrades between plans, tiers, and SKUs, with a focus on Azure Functions Consumption to Flex Consumption migration.
skill.summary: Assess Azure Functions upgrade readiness and guide safer plan migration steps.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are mostly true in context: this skill is documentation that instructs an AI agent to generate and run Azure CLI, curl, and deployment commands. I found no prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration, but the skill can create, modify, deploy, download packages from, and delete Azure resources, so it should not be published without stronger execution safeguards.

high_findings.title: Agent-Guided Azure Resource Modification and Deletion
high_findings.description: The skill guides execution of Azure CLI commands that create a new function app, deploy code, restart apps, and delete function apps. The documents include confirmation language, but marketplace use would still expose users to high-impact cloud changes if an agent executes the steps with broad Azure permissions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain concrete Azure CLI commands for migration, deployment, restart, and deletion. The intent is legitimate Azure upgrade automation, but the operational impact is clearly high.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Configuration and Package Retrieval
high_findings.description: The automation guide collects app settings, reads AzureWebJobsStorage, lists managed identity role assignments, and downloads a deployment package using a storage connection string. These steps can expose secrets or proprietary application code in the workspace or command output.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly retrieve app settings, identity data, and deployment packages. This is expected for migration work, but it creates real exposure risk if logs or files are mishandled.

medium_findings.title: Network Calls to User Workload Endpoints
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to call the default Azure Functions hostname and HTTP trigger endpoints with curl. This is a normal validation step, but it can contact production endpoints and may trigger application behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The curl commands and validation step are explicit. The risk depends on the target app behavior, so this is moderate rather than inherently malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local Workspace File Creation for Upgrade Artifacts
medium_findings.description: The skill requires creating status and assessment files in the workspace and can download a deployment package. This is useful for migration tracking, but it may persist sensitive configuration or application artifacts locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files and downloaded package are specifically named. The sensitivity depends on what values the agent writes into reports or package files.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Backtick Hits Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Many weak cryptography and Ruby backtick findings map to Markdown emphasis, diagrams, inline code, Azure SKU names, or shell examples. I found no executable Ruby code, hidden cryptographic implementation, or prompt-injection instruction in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are documentation constructs or code-fenced templates, not executable skill code. The broader command examples remain risky, but these specific pattern classes are false positives.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Benign References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft Learn and Azure sample documentation. I did not find evidence that the skill sends user data or secrets to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are cited as documentation links, not request targets for collected data. Network activity in the skill comes from Azure CLI and curl validation commands instead.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Command Execution Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains runnable Azure CLI and Functions Core Tools commands for login, subscription selection, migration, deployment, validation, restart, and deletion.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and intended to be run as part of the workflow. They are legitimate DevOps commands but dangerous without human approval and least-privilege Azure access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret-Bearing Configuration Collection
dangerous_patterns.description: The automation flow reads app settings and a storage connection string, then uses the connection string to list and download deployment blobs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands retrieve values that commonly include secrets and use them to access storage. No exfiltration target is shown, but accidental disclosure is a concrete risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Endpoint Probing
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation steps use curl against the upgraded app default host and function endpoint, which may invoke real production code paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct endpoint probes. They are common smoke tests, but risk varies with the target function implementation.

content.value_statement: Azure plan upgrades can fail when compatibility, identity, and deployment details are missed. This skill guides assessment, migration planning, execution steps, and validation for Azure Functions upgrades.
actual_capabilities: Guides Azure Functions Consumption to Flex Consumption assessment steps.
Creates a structured upgrade assessment report outline.
Lists Azure CLI checks for regions, runtimes, slots, certificates, triggers, and settings.
Provides migration commands for creating a new Flex Consumption function app.
Guides deployment method selection and post-upgrade validation.
Documents rollback, cleanup, and handoff steps after migration.
limitations: Focused mainly on Linux Azure Functions Consumption to Flex Consumption migration.
Requires Azure CLI, resource permissions, and user confirmation for real changes.
Does not automatically rewrite application code or infrastructure templates.
Some Azure platform details can change and should be checked against current Microsoft documentation.

use_cases.title: Assess Flex Consumption Readiness
use_cases.description: Check whether an existing Linux Consumption function app can move to Flex Consumption before making changes.

use_cases.title: Plan a Controlled Function App Upgrade
use_cases.description: Create an upgrade plan that covers app settings, identities, deployment choices, validation, and rollback.

use_cases.title: Validate a Completed Migration
use_cases.description: Run smoke tests, review migrated settings, and identify follow-up work after the new app is deployed.

prompt_templates.title: Start an Upgrade Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess my Azure Functions app for a move from Consumption to Flex Consumption. Ask me for the app name, resource group, subscription, and region first.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Migration Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a step-by-step upgrade checklist for my Linux Azure Functions app. Include compatibility checks, identity review, deployment options, validation, and rollback.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Azure CLI Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate Azure CLI commands for a Consumption to Flex Consumption migration. Do not run commands. Mark every destructive or production-impacting step for approval.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Failed Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this failed Azure Functions Flex Consumption migration output. Identify the failed phase, likely cause, safe recovery action, and any data I should protect.

output_examples.input: Assess whether my function app can move to Flex Consumption.
output: A readiness report with current plan, target plan, runtime, operating system, and region.
Compatibility checks for slots, certificates, blob triggers, runtime support, and identity configuration.
A list of blockers, warnings, recommendations, and next steps before migration.

output_examples.input: Help me upgrade after the assessment is approved.
output: A phased migration plan that creates a new app instead of replacing the old one.
Operator-reviewed Azure CLI commands for migration, configuration review, deployment, and validation.
Rollback and cleanup guidance that keeps the original app available until validation is complete.

output_examples.input: Validate the new app after deployment.
output: Smoke test steps for reachability and HTTP trigger checks.
Review points for migrated settings, identity, custom domains, monitoring, and scale configuration.
Recommendations for updating clients, pipelines, infrastructure definitions, and cleanup timing.

best_practices: Run assessment first and resolve blockers before creating or changing Azure resources.
Use least-privilege Azure access and require explicit approval before production-impacting commands.
Prefer managed identities over connection strings and redact secrets from reports and logs.
anti_patterns: Running generated Azure CLI commands without reviewing the target subscription and resource group.
Deleting or stopping the original app before the upgraded app is validated.
Writing full app settings, connection strings, or downloaded packages into shared workspaces.

faq.question: What Azure upgrade path does this skill cover best?
faq.answer: It is strongest for Linux Azure Functions Consumption plan upgrades to Flex Consumption.

faq.question: Can it run the migration automatically?
faq.answer: It provides automation steps, but real Azure changes should require explicit human approval.

faq.question: Does it migrate application code?
faq.answer: No. It explains deployment choices after the new app is created and configured.

faq.question: Does it support Windows Consumption apps?
faq.answer: The included scenario focuses on Linux. Windows support is listed as future platform support.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: The referenced workflow expects Azure CLI access and Owner or Contributor permissions in the target resource group.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill guides commands that can change cloud resources, read sensitive settings, download packages, and delete apps.
